nativeRange.habitat: Freshwater rivers, streams, and floodplain pools; typically found in slow-moving or still water with dense aquatic vegetation and soft substrate
nativeRange.biotope: Southeast Asian freshwater — slow-moving or still vegetated waters; soft muddy substrate with leaf litter and aquatic plants

decorPreferences: dense live planting (Java fern, Vallisneria, Amazon sword, floating plants)
multiple visual barriers and broken sightlines to reduce aggression
driftwood and caves for hiding and territory delineation
pest snail colony (ramshorn, bladder snails) for beak wear and enrichment
soft substrate — fine sand or smooth gravel
gentle filtration — sponge filter preferred to avoid strong flow

diet.notes: Primarily invertebrate prey — live or frozen bloodworms, blackworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, and tubifex are all accepted. Pest snails (ramshorn, bladder snails, pond snails) are essential for natural beak wear and should be offered regularly; without hard-shelled food, beak/tooth overgrowth (“lockjaw”) can occur. Frozen mussels, cockles, and shrimp meat are useful for variety. May be reluctant to accept dried or prepared foods on arrival; live foods often needed to initiate feeding. Pipette or spot-feeding is recommended to ensure individual fish receive adequate rations.

goodWith: conspecifics only in very large, heavily planted tanks with multiple territories (species-only setup strongly preferred)
fast-moving mid-water species in very large tanks may be tolerated with careful monitoring
avoidWith: long-finned or slow-moving fish (bettas, guppies, angelfish, fancy goldfish) — severe fin-nipping risk
shrimp and small invertebrates — will be hunted and eaten
other pufferfish species — interspecific aggression likely
small or delicate fish that cannot escape harassment
snails intended as permanent tank inhabitants — will be consumed

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Carinotetraodon lorteti; verify availability and licensing before use.

summary: A small (~5 cm) true freshwater puffer from Southeast Asia distinguished by vivid red irises and strong male–female dichromatism. Aggressive and best kept singly or in species-only setups; requires hard-shelled invertebrates regularly for beak maintenance.
careNotes:
The Red-Eye Puffer (Carinotetraodon lorteti) is a true freshwater puffer native to Southeast Asia, found in Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, Malaysia, and parts of Indonesia. At roughly 4–5.5 cm adult length it is noticeably larger than the closely related Pea Puffer (C. travancoricus, max ~3.5 cm) and can be distinguished by its more robust build and the striking sexual dichromatism: males develop intense red or orange irises and bold patterning, while females are comparatively drab with a pale spotted pattern.

This species is a true obligate freshwater fish and should never be kept in brackish or marine conditions; do not confuse with euryhaline tetraodontid puffers that require salt.

Aggression is the primary husbandry challenge. C. lorteti is highly aggressive toward conspecifics and will readily attack and injure or kill tankmates. Solo keeping in a dedicated species tank is strongly recommended. If attempting a species-only group, a very large, heavily planted tank (150 L+) with multiple broken sightlines and individual territories is required; females-only groups or one male with multiple females have been attempted by specialists with mixed results. Any cohabitation should be monitored closely for bite wounds.

Fin-nipping is a severe risk to any long-finned or slow-moving tankmate. C. lorteti should not be housed with bettas, guppies, fancy goldfish, angelfish, or similar species.

Beak/tooth overgrowth (“lockjaw”) is a documented welfare issue when puffers are denied hard-shelled prey. A live pest snail culture (ramshorn, bladder snails) should be maintained as a permanent food source. Frozen shellfish (cockle, mussel) and hard-shelled invertebrates further supplement beak wear.

Dichromatism note: The vivid red eye coloration is most pronounced in healthy, well-fed males in good water conditions. Faded coloration can indicate stress, poor water quality, or nutritional deficiency.

Sexing: Males are larger, more colorful, and have dark patterning on the belly; females are smaller with a pale cream-to-yellow spotted pattern. Juveniles are difficult to sex.

Water quality: Like all puffers, C. lorteti is sensitive to ammonia and nitrite. Frequent partial water changes (30–50% weekly) and robust biological filtration are important; the species produces significant waste relative to its size. Nitrates should be kept below 20 ppm where possible.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been documented but is uncommon and considered moderately difficult. A species-only setup with one male and one or more females is required. Courtship involves the male chasing and displaying to the female; spawning occurs on flat surfaces, plant leaves, or fine substrate. Eggs are small and adhesive; the male may or may not guard them depending on individual temperament. Eggs hatch in approximately 5–7 days at 26°C. Fry are very small and require infusoria or paramecia initially, progressing to baby brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Separating eggs or fry from adults improves survival rates. Rearing fry to adulthood requires dedicated effort and clean, well-oxygenated water.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Carinotetraodon lorteti
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, water parameters (temperature, pH, hardness), native range (Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, Malaysia), habitat description, diet (invertebrates, snails), sexual dichromatism, aggression notes, and species-only tank recommendation.

additional.name: FishBase — Carinotetraodon lorteti
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Tetraodontidae, order Tetraodontiformes), adult size (max ~5 cm TL), native range (Mekong and Chao Phraya basin drainages, Southeast Asia), temperature range, demersal freshwater habitat.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Puffer Fish Care
additional.notes: General puffer care guide; confirmed carnivore diet requirements (snails for beak wear, live/frozen invertebrate foods), aggression management, species-only tank recommendation, and freshwater true puffer husbandry guidance applicable to C. lorteti.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Carinotetraodon lorteti species profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked sexual dichromatism (vivid red eyes and bold pattern in males vs. drab spotted females), aggression level, tank size guidance, diet (snails, frozen invertebrates), water parameters, and SE Asian native range.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Dwarf Puffers of the Genus Carinotetraodon
additional.notes: Provided genus-level context distinguishing C. lorteti from C. travancoricus (larger size, stronger dichromatism, SE Asian vs. Indian range), aggression comparisons within genus, beak overgrowth risk, and captive breeding notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-layer; spawning in captivity documented but uncommon. Eggs laid on flat surfaces or plant leaves; male may guard. Fry require infusoria then baby brine shrimp nauplii. Separation of eggs or fry from adults improves yield.
